summary: The paper contains one strong empirical contribution (H3: downstream correlation) surrounded by two cleanly-reported negative results (H1: LV detector, H2: PMI) and one inflated exploratory result (taxonomy). The downstream correlation analysis is well-executed but suffers from a critical confound: the five highest-absorption SAEs are ALL 1M-width, while the five lowest are ALL 16k/65k. Width is the dominant confounder, and the partial correlation claim of 'strengthening after controlling for width' must be scrutinized because log(width) is a coarse 3-level categorical factor (16k/65k/1M) that may not adequately remove the nonlinear width effect. The taxonomy's 92.3% headline figure is misleading given the paper's own acknowledgment that Type II identification is invalid. The paper is honest about most failures but still overclaims in the abstract and conclusion by presenting the 92.3% figure without immediate qualification.

findings.description: H3 downstream correlation confounded by width. All 5 high-absorption SAEs in matched RAVEL comparison (C3B) are 1M-width; all 5 low-absorption SAEs are 16k/65k. The absorption-performance correlation may be substantially driven by a width-quality relationship (1M SAEs have lower quality AND higher absorption) rather than absorption causing lower quality. Partial correlations control for log(width) as a continuous variable, but this only removes the linear component of the width effect; 1M SAEs are qualitatively different (64x wider than 65k) and may operate in a fundamentally different regime.
findings.suggestion: Run stratified correlations within each width tier (16k-only, 65k-only, 1M-only). If the correlation disappears within tiers, the claim is an ecological fallacy. Also compute partial correlations using width as a 3-level categorical dummy rather than log(width). Report both analyses.

findings.description: Type II taxonomy rate (88.5%) is invalid as an absorption measurement. The paper itself flags this in evidence_quality.suspicious_flags: parent features were identified by a selectivity heuristic, NOT sae-spelling ground truth. The magnitude ratio compares to a global mean-when-active with n_comparison_tokens=0 for most letters. The resulting 92.3% comprehensive rate is an upper bound driven by a measurement artifact, not by absorption. Yet the abstract states '92.3% of letter features as showing some absorption' without immediate qualification, and the conclusion repeats '92.3% comprehensive absorption rate' as a finding.
findings.suggestion: Demote the 92.3% figure in the abstract and conclusion. Replace with: 'Up to 92.3%, though the Type II component (88.5%) is likely inflated by heuristic parent identification and requires validation.' In the abstract, report only Type I (3.8%) and the Chanin any-absorption rate (80.8%) as validated figures.

findings.description: Model mismatch between detection experiments and downstream analysis undermines the paper's coherence. H1/H2/H4/taxonomy are all on GPT-2 Small; H3 is on Gemma Scope SAEs. The paper cannot compose these into a single narrative. The proposal originally targeted Gemma 2 2B throughout, but pivoted to GPT-2 Small due to HuggingFace gating. This is not just a limitation—it means the paper's strongest result (H3) and its most novel contribution (taxonomy) cannot be linked on the same model.
findings.suggestion: Explicitly state in Section 1 that the paper reports two independent analyses on different models rather than an integrated study. Move the model-mismatch caveat from Section 7.5 to Section 1 so readers see it before reaching the results.

findings.description: Safety probe experiment (C3C) is fundamentally confounded and should be presented as pilot-only. The three SAEs differ in layer (5, 8, 10), width (768, 24k, 32k), architecture family, AND hook point. The probe gap is 0.118 (lowest abs), 0.148 (median), 0.051 (highest abs)—inverse of the predicted direction. The paper correctly notes this is confounded, but still presents the result in a main table (Table 6). With n=3 SAEs, n=100 prompts, and 3+ confounds, this experiment has zero statistical power to test the absorption-safety relationship.
findings.suggestion: Move Table 6 to appendix. Reduce the safety probe to a paragraph noting it was attempted but confounded and underpowered. Do not present it as a main result.

findings.description: LV detector evaluation uses an anomalous bin structure that obscures interpretation. The first alpha_ij bin [0, 0.1] has n=369 pairs with absorption rate 0.848 — by far the highest rate. The paper explains this as 'related latents both absorbed by a common parent' but this means the alpha_ij computation is systematically anti-correlated with absorption at low values. The ROC-AUC of 0.148 (below 0.5 = random) confirms the detector is worse than random—it anti-predicts absorption. This is more informative than the F1=0.128 framing and should be highlighted.
findings.suggestion: Report the ROC-AUC < 0.5 finding prominently. A detector that anti-predicts is more interesting than one that merely fails. Discuss why low alpha_ij systematically identifies absorbed pairs (because absorbed parents have suppressed frequency, reducing f_i, but their children have high f_j, and co-activation is low because the parent is already suppressed—so alpha_ij is paradoxically low for absorbed pairs).

findings.description: Decoder cosine pre-filter at 0.15 creates a hard recall ceiling of 34%. This means 66% of absorbed pairs are excluded from the candidate set before alpha_ij is even computed. The LV detector cannot possibly achieve F1 > 0.34 even with a perfect classifier on the filtered set. The paper reports this in Section 6.2 but does not adequately discuss its implication: the entire H1 evaluation is ceiling-limited by a design choice, not by the theoretical framework.
findings.suggestion: Report the oracle F1 achievable within the filtered set (i.e., if the LV threshold perfectly classified all filtered pairs). If this oracle F1 is also low, the problem is with alpha_ij; if it is high, the problem is with the pre-filter. This disambiguates two failure modes.

findings.description: The abstract claims the paper 'establishes absorption as a validated quality indicator that current unsupervised methods cannot reliably detect.' This is overclaiming. The correlational evidence (H3) establishes association, not validation in a causal sense. 'Validated quality indicator' implies interventional evidence (reduce absorption, observe quality improvement) that does not exist. The correlation could be driven by a common cause (e.g., SAE training quality affects both absorption and downstream performance).
findings.suggestion: Replace 'validated quality indicator' with 'correlational quality indicator' or 'quality-associated metric.' Add a sentence explicitly noting that causal evidence would require intervention experiments.

findings.description: SCR partial correlation jump from r=-0.431 to partial r=-0.677 is a suppressor effect red flag. The paper notes this ('partial correlations can inflate under suppressor effects') but does not investigate. A jump from |r|=0.43 to |r|=0.68 after partialing out 3 covariates at n=49 is unusually large and warrants specific diagnosis of which covariate acts as a suppressor.
findings.suggestion: Report which covariate(s) cause the suppressor effect by sequentially adding covariates. Compute partial r controlling for each covariate individually. If the suppressor is width (plausible given the confound structure), this further undermines the causal interpretation.

findings.description: The paper's strongest finding—that SAEBench absorption scores correlate with downstream metrics—is a straightforward correlation analysis on pre-existing public data (SAEBench CSV). While no prior paper published this analysis, any researcher with access to SAEBench data could compute it in an afternoon. The finding is valuable but the novelty bar for a NeurIPS paper is not met by a correlation matrix alone. The two novel methodological contributions (LV detector, PMI predictor) both failed.
findings.suggestion: Reframe the paper honestly: the two novel methodological hypotheses were falsified (negative results), and the main positive finding is an overlooked correlation in public data. This is publishable as a short empirical paper (ICLR tiny papers, NeurIPS workshop) but the claimed contributions need recalibration for a main-track NeurIPS submission.

findings.description: The GPT-2 Small SAE configuration survey (C2B) uses 31 configurations across 3 release families with different training objectives and hook points. The regression treats all 806 (config, letter) observations as independent, but letters within a configuration are correlated (same SAE), and configurations within a family share training methodology. Neither level of clustering is addressed.
findings.suggestion: Fit a mixed-effects model with random intercepts for letter and configuration. Report whether the layer and L0 significance survives clustering. If not, the PMI null result is confirmed but the 'layer is a strong predictor' claim is weakened.

findings.description: The DAS(k=3) metric is estimated via McFadden pseudo-R^2 of logistic regression on n=10,000 tokens with k=3 predictors. At 24k SAE width, n_word_act_samples is only 40 per letter (not 10,000 as stated in the methodology). The actual activation samples used for DAS estimation are unclear—possibly 40 words times some number of tokens per word, but the methodology claims 10k tokens.
findings.suggestion: Clarify the actual sample size for DAS estimation. If it is indeed ~40 per letter (as n_word_act_samples suggests), the logistic regression is likely overfitting and DAS values are unreliable. Report confidence intervals on DAS estimates via bootstrap.

findings.description: The paper title 'When Features Compete' implies the competitive exclusion framework is supported, but the paper's main finding is that it is NOT supported. The title creates a misleading first impression that the ecological analogy works.
findings.suggestion: Consider a title that foregrounds the actual findings: e.g., 'Feature Absorption Predicts SAE Quality But Resists Unsupervised Detection: Empirical Tests of Competition, Corpus Statistics, and Downstream Impact.'

findings.description: Cross-architecture validation (C1C) tests against OpenAI v5 SAEs which use resid_post (vs resid_pre for the baseline). This is not just an architecture change—it is a different activation space entirely. The paper notes this but still claims 'LV detector does not generalize across architectures.' A fairer test would compare within the same hook point but across training objectives.
findings.suggestion: Qualify the cross-architecture finding: the LV detector does not generalize across hook points AND architectures simultaneously. The two confounds cannot be separated.

findings.description: The related work section (2.2) includes a speculative claim about why JumpReLU shows highest absorption ('consistent with longer training amplifying sparsity-gradient dynamics'). This is not supported by evidence in this paper and is speculation presented as hypothesis without basis.
findings.suggestion: Remove or qualify with 'one possible explanation, which we do not test, is...' or simply note the empirical finding without mechanistic speculation.

metric_gaming_risks: Type II taxonomy rate (88.5%) appears to game the 'comprehensive absorption is higher than reported' narrative by using a measurement procedure known to be invalid (heuristic parent identification with zero comparison tokens). The paper's own evidence_quality.suspicious_flags acknowledge this.

The H3 downstream correlation could be driven entirely by the width confound (1M SAEs have high absorption AND low quality), creating an illusory absorption-quality link. Without within-width-tier validation, the correlation could be an artifact of including 1M SAEs.

The '92.3% comprehensive rate vs 15-35% canonical rate' comparison is misleading because these use different measurement methodologies on different models. The canonical 15-35% is from Chanin et al. on Gemma; the 92.3% is on GPT-2 with a different (weaker) measurement approach.

The matched RAVEL comparison (Cohen's d=2.13) compares groups that differ not only in absorption but also in width (16k/65k vs 1M) and L0 (137-297 vs 16-58). The massive effect size reflects these confounds, not just absorption.
novelty_risks: The main positive finding (H3 correlation) is a straightforward statistical analysis on publicly available SAEBench data. The analysis is simple (Pearson/Spearman correlation with Bonferroni correction) and could be reproduced by anyone with access to the data.

The LV competitive exclusion framework, which is the paper's most novel theoretical contribution, is conclusively falsified (F1=0.128, ROC-AUC=0.148). The paper contributes the formalization and the negative result, but this is a narrower contribution than the proposal envisioned.

The three-tier taxonomy is conceptually straightforward (full/partial/distributed) and the operationalization is acknowledged as problematic (Type II inflation). The taxonomy definition is novel but the measurement is not validated.
reproducibility_gaps: GPT-2 Small SAE loading via SAELens release names (gpt2-small-res-jb, gpt2-small-res-jb-feature-splitting, gpt2-small-resid-post-v5) is reproducible but version-sensitive. No SAELens version pin is specified.
The sae-spelling integration is not described in sufficient detail. Which version of sae-spelling? Which specific command or function call?
PMI computation uses 'sliding window of 5 tokens' but the actual implementation details (how joint probability is estimated, how tokens are matched to letters) are not specified in the paper.
The safety probe dataset (50 harmful + 50 benign prompts) is not released. Categories and specific prompts are not listed.
SAEBench data was loaded from HuggingFace (adamkarvonen/sae_bench_results). The specific version/commit of this dataset is not pinned.
